perturbation.original_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means and includes all data, documents and information disclosed or to be disclosed by either Party to other Party and its Representatives (as defined herein) (including i nformation transmitted in written, electronic, magnetic or other form and also information transmitted orally or visually) and any and all information which may be developed or created, in whole or in part, directly or indirectly, from such information including all notes, summaries, an alyses, compilations and other writings.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Confidential Information” means and includes only the specific data, documents, and information explicitly marked as confidential at the time of disclosure by either Party to the other Party and its Representatives (as defined herein) (including information transmitted in written form only). It excludes any information developed or created from such disclosed data.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Confidential Information' is narrowed to include only explicitly marked written information, excluding oral or visual transmissions and any derived information. This contradicts the broader definition later in the text, where 'Confidential Information' is referenced more generically, potentially leading to disputes over what constitutes confidential data. The change directly impacts Section 6 and Section 8.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 6. All Confidential Information is provided “as it is”. In no event shall Disclosing Party be liable for the inaccuracy or incompleteness of the Confid ential Information. None of the Confidential Information disclosed by the parties constitutes any representation, warranty, assurance, guarantee or inducement by either party to the other with respect to the fitness of such Confidential Information for any particular purpose or infringement of trademarks, patents, copyrights or any right of third parties.
perturbation.justification: The changed text significantly narrows the definition of 'Confidential Information' to only explicitly marked written information, excluding oral or visual transmissions and any derived information. This directly contradicts Section 6, which refers to 'All Confidential Information' being provided 'as is' without limitation to the specific criteria introduced in the changed definition. Section 6 implies a broader scope of confidential information, encompassing more than just explicitly marked written data. Therefore, the new definition creates a direct conflict in what constitutes 'Confidential Information' within the contract, leading to a strong in-text contradiction as defined in 'Ambiguities – In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Receiving Party acknowledges that Confidential Information is valuable, proprietary and confidential. Unless other wise expressly authorized by Disclosing Party, Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence for a period as stipulated in Clause 7 herein, and shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party or any employee or agent (except its officers, employees, representatives, advisors or agents who have a need to know) and shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the purposes agree to herein. No license expressed or implied in the Confi dential Information is granted to either party other than to use the information in the minor and to the extent authorised by this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Receiving Party acknowledges that Confidential Information *may* be valuable, proprietary, and confidential. Unless otherwise expressly authorized by Disclosing Party, Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence for a *reasonable* period, not to exceed 30 days, and shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party or any employee or agent (except its officers, employees, representatives, advisors or agents who have a need to know) and *may* use the Confidential Information for purposes of internal evaluation only. No license expressed or implied in the Confidential Information is granted to either party other than to use the information in the minor and to the extent authorised by this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation weakens the obligation to protect 'Confidential Information' by changing 'is' to 'may be', replacing the defined retention period with a vague 'reasonable period' (capped at 30 days), and limiting usage to 'internal evaluation only'. This directly contradicts the intention and defined time period, which creates an in-text contradiction with Section 7, which talks about confidentiality being binding without a time limit until it becomes public knowledge.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. This Agreement shall be effective from the date hereof and shall terminate upon the completion of any transaction in relation to the underlying transacti on or otherwise as determined by the parties; however, the obligations of Receiving Party respecting disclosure and confidentiality shall continue to be binding and applicable without limit in time except and, until such information enters the public domai n save and except the clause (2) herein.
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces several contradictions. First, replacing 'is valuable' with 'may be valuable' weakens the confidentiality obligation. Second, changing the defined retention period in Clause 7 to a 'reasonable period, not to exceed 30 days' directly conflicts with Section 7, which states confidentiality obligations continue 'without limit in time'. Finally, limiting the use of confidential information to 'internal evaluation only' contradicts the potential for broader use implied by the original text and Section 7. These changes create ambiguity and inconsistency regarding the duration and scope of confidentiality, establishing a strong in-text contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Receiving Party agrees, except to the extent pursuant to, or required by law, regulation, legal process or regula tory authority to disclose any Confidential Information (prior intimation be provided to Disclosing Party to enable it to take recourse of legal remedies viz, injunctions, stay etc.), Receiving Party shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Inform ation for any purpose not contemplated by this Agreement; or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Receiving Party agrees that it *may*, at its sole discretion, disclose Confidential Information to any of its affiliates, subsidiaries, or parent companies without prior intimation to Disclosing Party, provided it believes such disclosure is in its best interest. Receiving Party shall not (i) use any portion of the Confidential Information for any direct commercial purpose competitive with the Disclosing Party; or (ii) disclose the Confidential Information or any part of it to any third party.
perturbation.explanation: This change allows the Receiving Party to disclose 'Confidential Information' to related entities without notifying the Disclosing Party, creating an exception that undermines the core purpose of the NDA. This contradicts the general prohibition against disclosure outlined in Section 3 and, specifically, the requirement for prior intimation before disclosure. This change also, contradicts Section 12, which requires any kind of releases to be approved by the other party.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 12. Neither party shall make news releases, public announcement s, give interviews, issue or publish advertisements or publicize in any other manner whatsoever in connectio n with this Agreement , the content s / provision s thereof , other information relating to this Agreement, the Purpose, the Confidential Information or other matter of this Agreement, without the prior written approval of the other party.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 4 allows the Receiving Party to disclose Confidential Information to affiliated entities without prior notification, which directly contradicts Section 12's requirement for prior written approval from the other party before making any public announcements or disclosures related to the Agreement, its contents, or the Confidential Information. This creates a strong in-text contradiction because the Receiving Party could disclose confidential information to an affiliate, which could then publicize it without the Disclosing Party's prior approval, violating Section 12.

perturbation.original_text: 7. This Agreement shall be effective from the date hereof and shall terminate upon the completion of any transaction in relation to the underlying transacti on or otherwise as determined by the parties; however, the obligations of Receiving Party respecting disclosure and confidentiality shall continue to be binding and applicable without limit in time except and, until such information enters the public domai n save and except the clause (2) herein.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. This Agreement shall be effective from the date hereof and shall terminate automatically one (1) year from this date, irrespective of the completion of any transaction. The obligations of Receiving Party respecting disclosure and confidentiality shall terminate concurrently with the agreement's termination, except as explicitly extended in a separate written agreement signed by both parties.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the agreement's term from being tied to the transaction's completion to a fixed one-year period. This directly contradicts the original clause that obligations continue 'without limit in time' until the information becomes public. This introduces ambiguity on what the actual term and conditions of the agreement are. This change introduces an In-Text Contradiction by changing the agreement from being applicable without limit in time and until information becomes public to a year.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 3. Receiving Party acknowledges that Confidential Information is valuable, proprietary and confidential. Unless other wise expressly authorized by Disclosing Party, Receiving Party agrees to retain the Confidential Information in confidence for a period as stipulated in Clause 7 herein, and shall not disclose the Confidential Information to any third party or any employee or agent (except its officers, employees, representatives, advisors or agents who have a need to know) and shall not use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than for the purposes agree to herein. No license expressed or implied in the Confi dential Information is granted to either party other than to use the information in the minor and to the extent authorised by this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The original text in Clause 7 states that confidentiality obligations continue 'without limit in time'. The changed text in Clause 7 introduces a one-year termination, contradicting the 'without limit in time' obligation. Clause 3 states that the Receiving Party must retain Confidential Information as stipulated in Clause 7. With the change, Clause 7 now states one year, which directly contradicts and violates the agreement made in Clause 3, because both clauses can not exist together. This presents a clear and impactful contradiction that would cause confusion, unenforceability, or a legal issue within the document.

perturbation.original_text: 8. Receiving Party agrees to return to Disclosing Party upon its written request, the Confidential Information, any copies of the Confidential Information (whether authorised or not) and any documents or items conta ining portions of the Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. Receiving Party is *encouraged* to return to Disclosing Party upon its written request, the Confidential Information, any copies of the Confidential Information (whether authorized or not) and any documents or items containing portions of the Confidential Information; however, the Receiving Party may retain one archival copy for legal compliance purposes.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'agrees to return' to 'is encouraged to return' weakens the obligation, making it optional. Allowing retention of an archival copy 'for legal compliance' without defining what constitutes such compliance introduces ambiguity and could allow for broader interpretation and retention. This weakens the Disclosing Party's control over their confidential information and contradicts Section 5 where no rights are granted under any copyright.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Notwithstanding the disclosure of any Confidential Informat ion by the Disclosing Party to Rece iving Party, the Disclosing Party shall retain title and all intellectual property and proprietary rights in the Confidential Information. No license under any trade mark, patents or copy right, or application for same which are now or thereafter may be ob tained by Disclosing Party is either granted or implied by the conveying of Confidential Information . The Receiving Party shall not conceal, alter, obliterate, mutilate, deface or otherwise interfere with any trade mark, trade mark notice, copy right notic e, confidentiality notice or any notice of any other proprietary right of the Disclosing Party on any copy of the Confidential Information, and shall reproduce any such mark or notice on all copies of such Confidential Information. Likewise, the Receiving Party shall not add or emboss its own or any other mark, symbol or logo on such Confidential Information.
perturbation.justification: The changed text weakens the obligation to return confidential information from 'agrees to return' to 'is encouraged to return,' making it optional. This directly conflicts with Section 5, which states that the Disclosing Party retains all intellectual property rights and no rights are granted or implied by conveying Confidential Information. Furthermore, allowing the Receiving Party to retain an archival copy 'for legal compliance purposes' without defining what constitutes such compliance introduces ambiguity and could allow for broader interpretation and retention, directly contradicting the Disclosing Party's control over its confidential information as outlined in Section 5.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Any dispute, controv ersy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or any related agreement or other document or the validity, interpretation, breach or termination thereof ( Dispute'), including claims seeking redress or asserting rights under applicable law, shal l, be resolved amicably between the parties and in case the dispute could not be settled by mutual negotiation, then the same shall be referred to a sole arbitrator appointed by the Bank and the decision of the Arbitrator shall be final and bi nding on the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Any dispute, controv ersy or claim arising out of or relating to this Agreement or any related agreement or other document or the validity, interpretation, breach or termination thereof ( Dispute'), including claims seeking redress or asserting rights under applicable law, *may* be resolved amicably between the parties; however, either party reserves the right to pursue legal remedies in a court of competent jurisdiction as their first course of action. Arbitration is explicitly waived.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the mandatory arbitration clause and allows either party to immediately pursue legal action. This directly contradicts the original intent of resolving disputes through arbitration as the primary method. This change creates an In-Text Contradiction by not specifying if the competent court is in Bangalore, as it originally indicates in Section 11.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 11. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of India, and the competent courts in Bangalore shall have jurisdiction in relation thereto.
perturbation.justification: The original text mandated arbitration for disputes. The changed text removes mandatory arbitration, explicitly waives it, and allows for legal remedies in a court of competent jurisdiction as a first course of action. Section 11 states that competent courts in Bangalore have jurisdiction. By waiving arbitration entirely and not specifying the court's location in the changed text, there's a direct contradiction, as the agreement no longer adheres to the Bangalore jurisdiction, leading to a strong contradiction.
